worth_it_explanation: User access and API credentials decide who can change a publisher's Index Exchange (IX) configuration, read its revenue data, and call its programmatic interfaces. An account where every user holds admin rights, where a departed staffer still has a login, or where a shared API token never rotates widens the surface for a costly mistake or a misused credential. The check is a review of the access already granted in the IX console rather than a rebuild, so it costs reading time, and the finding points to specific accounts and keys the publisher can tighten.
intro: Access governance covers who can sign in to the IX publisher console, what each person can do once inside, and how the programmatic credentials that call IX interfaces are scoped and maintained. This subcategory looks at two things, the human accounts and their permission levels, and the API credentials or access tokens that automated systems use. These checks confirm that access follows least privilege, that people hold only the rights their job needs, that accounts for departed staff are removed, and that shared or long-lived credentials are scoped narrowly and rotated rather than left standing.

audit_checks.title: Console user roles do not follow least privilege
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When every user holds broad admin rights and departed staff retain active logins, one mistaken change or one misused login can alter pricing, demand partner settings, or payout details across the account. Granting more access than a role needs widens the blast radius of any error or compromise without adding any operational benefit.

educate.base:
Least privilege is the practice of giving each user only the access their job requires and no more. The principle is a long-standing security control. The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) defines it as restricting access privileges to the minimum necessary to accomplish assigned tasks. In an Index Exchange (IX) publisher console, that means a person who reads revenue reports holds a read-only or reporting role, a person who tunes pricing holds the rights to do that and not to change payout banking details, and only a small number of trusted people hold full admin rights.

The reason this matters in an SSP console is the breadth of what an admin can change. Account-level settings reach pricing rules, demand partner relationships, inventory configuration, and payment information, so an account where everyone is an admin turns every login into a single point of failure. A mistaken edit, a phishing-compromised password, or a disgruntled former contributor each carries the same broad reach as the most senior operator. Narrowing roles to match actual responsibilities keeps a single mistake or a single stolen credential contained to a smaller area.

The departed-staff problem is the other half of this check. People change roles and leave companies and agencies, and an access list that is never pruned accumulates logins that no longer map to anyone who should be reaching the account. Each standing account for a person who has moved on is a credential the publisher is no longer watching, which is exactly the kind of access an attacker looks for.

A few signs point to access that does not follow least privilege:
  • Most or all console users hold full admin rights regardless of what their work requires
  • Active logins exist for people who have left the company, the agency, or the project
  • No record shows when access was last reviewed or who approved each user's role
You may want to confirm that each console user holds the narrowest role their work requires and that accounts for departed staff have been removed. One approach is to list every active user with their role, match each to a current responsibility, and revoke or downgrade any that no longer fit.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media property, access often spreads across an internal ad ops team, editorial or commercial staff who want reporting, and one or more external partners such as a monetization agency. You may want to give the reporting-only people a read-only role, reserve configuration rights for the ad ops team, and confirm that any agency access is scoped to what the engagement covers and removed when it ends.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content property, the people who care about ad revenue may sit alongside a separate web or marketing team that does not need console access at all. You may want to keep the user list limited to the staff and partners who actually operate the IX setup, with reporting-only roles for anyone who just needs the numbers, rather than extending admin access across a broader team.

educate.learn_more_note: Index Exchange's detailed documentation for console user roles and permission levels is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the list of active console users, the role assigned to each, and whether any belong to people who have left, then match each role against the access the person's work actually requires.

ix_console.label: Index Exchange console
steps: Open the user management or account settings area of the Index Exchange publisher console and list every active user with the role assigned to each.
For each user, note whether the role is admin, a configuration role, or a reporting or read-only role, and record what level of access that role grants.
Match each user to a current responsibility, and flag any account that belongs to a person who has left the company, the agency, or the project.
Ask the account owner when access was last reviewed and who approves new users, and record whether any review process exists.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each active console user, the role assigned, and whether that role matches the person's current responsibility. A setup where roles follow least privilege, with admin rights held by a small set of trusted operators, reporting-only roles for people who just need data, and no active accounts for departed staff, passes. A setup where most users hold admin rights, or where logins for departed staff remain active, is a fail recorded with the accounts and roles that need to be revoked or downgraded.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each console user, their role, and whether it matches a current responsibility above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} grants Index Exchange (IX) console access more broadly than each user's work requires, with admin rights held widely and, in some cases, active logins for people who have left. Because account-level access in an SSP console reaches pricing, demand partner settings, and payment details, every broad login is a single point of failure for a mistaken change or a misused credential. {{voice_recommend}} listing every active user with their role, matching each to a current responsibility, and revoking or downgrading any account that holds more access than its work needs or that belongs to departed staff. After the cleanup, a short recurring access review keeps the user list aligned with who should reach the account.

audit_checks.title: API credentials are shared, broadly scoped, or never rotated
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An API token that is shared across systems, scoped to more than the integration needs, or never rotated stays valid long after the people who created it have moved on and is hard to trace to a single owner. A leaked or stale credential of this kind can read account data or drive automated changes until someone notices, and a shared key cannot be revoked without disrupting every system that depends on it.

educate.base:
Beyond human logins, an Index Exchange (IX) setup usually relies on programmatic credentials, an API key or access token that a reporting pipeline, a yield tool, or another automated system uses to call IX interfaces. These credentials deserve the same governance as user accounts, and often get less because they sit in config files and scheduled jobs rather than in front of a person who logs in each day.

Three patterns weaken an API credential. A shared credential, where one key is pasted into several systems, cannot be revoked when one of those systems is decommissioned or compromised without breaking all the others, and it cannot be traced to a single owner. A broadly scoped credential, granted more permissions than the integration uses, carries the same wide reach as a full admin if it leaks, so a key that only needs to read reports should not also be able to change configuration. A credential that never rotates stays valid indefinitely, which means a key copied into a repository or a former contractor's laptop years ago may still work today.

The remedies are well established in security guidance. The Open Worldwide Application Security Project (OWASP) recommends regularly rotating secrets so that any stolen credential works only for a short time, and applying least privilege so each system can reach only the secrets and scopes it needs. A separate, narrowly scoped credential per integration, rotated on a schedule, gives the publisher a key it can revoke and replace without collateral damage and a clear line from each credential to the system that owns it.

A few signs point to API credentials that need attention:
  • One API key or token is shared across multiple systems rather than one credential per integration
  • Credentials carry broad permissions, for example write or admin scope, when the integration only reads data
  • No rotation schedule exists and the publisher cannot say when each credential was last replaced
  • Credentials sit in plain text in code, shared documents, or chat history rather than in a secrets store
You may want to confirm that each integration uses its own narrowly scoped credential, that those credentials rotate on a schedule, and that none sit in plain text where they can leak. One approach is to inventory every API credential, the system that uses it, and its scope, then replace any shared, over-scoped, or stale key.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media property, reporting and yield-management credentials are often created once by an agency or a former team member and then left running untouched. You may want to inventory every IX API credential, confirm it is scoped to read-only where the system only pulls reports, and replace any key whose owner has changed or whose last rotation date no one can name.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content property, an analytics or business-intelligence pipeline may pull IX revenue data through an API credential alongside other data sources. You may want to confirm that credential is scoped only to reporting, kept out of any shared dashboard configuration where colleagues could read it, and rotated on the same schedule as the property's other data-source keys.

educate.learn_more_note: Index Exchange's documentation for generating and scoping API credentials and access tokens is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review every API credential or access token used to call Index Exchange interfaces, the system that uses each one, its scope, and when it was last rotated.

ix_console.label: Index Exchange console
steps: Open the API access or developer settings area of the Index Exchange publisher console and list every active API credential or access token.
For each credential, record the system that uses it, the scope or permissions it carries, and the creation or last-rotation date if shown.
Note any credential that appears to be shared across more than one system, that carries write or admin scope while the integration only reads data, or whose rotation date is old or unknown.

credential_inventory.label: Credential inventory review
steps: Ask the account owner where each IX API credential is stored, for example a secrets manager, a config file, a shared document, or chat history.
Record any credential that sits in plain text in code, a shared document, or a message thread rather than in a secrets store.
Confirm whether a rotation schedule exists and when each credential was last replaced, and flag any that has never rotated.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each API credential, the system that uses it, its scope, where it is stored, and its last rotation date. A setup where each integration has its own narrowly scoped credential, stored in a secrets manager, on a rotation schedule, passes. A setup with a shared key across systems, a credential scoped wider than its integration needs, a credential stored in plain text, or one with no rotation on record is a fail recorded with the specific credentials to replace or re-scope.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each API credential, its scope, storage location, and last rotation date above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} relies on Index Exchange (IX) API credentials that are shared across systems, scoped wider than the integration needs, or never rotated. A credential of this kind stays valid long after its owner has moved on and is hard to trace to a single system, so a leaked or stale key can read account data or drive changes until someone notices. {{voice_recommend}} giving each integration its own narrowly scoped credential, storing it in a secrets manager rather than plain text, and rotating it on a schedule, following the rotation and least-privilege guidance from the Open Worldwide Application Security Project (OWASP). After the change, a short inventory of each credential and its owner keeps the programmatic access as accountable as the human logins.
